name: Inpatient Bedside Rounds

source.author: TBD — Dr. [Director]
source.derivedFrom: Stanford GREAT/I-RCCC rounding deck + COACHING Inpatient Observation form (generalized).

description: DRAFT for the director to react to — NOT validated. Built on the GREAT mnemonic (Greeting, Respond to emotion via PEARLS, Elicit concerns, Assessment, Treatment plan) mapped onto the 5 universal phases. Family-centered: the patient AND family are present. Only transcript-detectable criteria included.

dimensions.label: Opening (Greeting)
dimensions.description: GREAT step G — greeting and introduction.

coaching.whyItMatters: A warm greeting on rounds signals respect and sets a relationship-centered tone with the patient and family.
examplePhrases: Good morning, how are you feeling today?
I'm Dr. ___, part of the team looking after you.

dimensions.label: Eliciting concerns (Elicit)
dimensions.description: GREAT step E — elicit the patient/family's concerns for the day.

coaching.whyItMatters: Asking 'what can we do for you today?' and 'what else?' puts the patient's agenda alongside the team's.
examplePhrases: Your concerns are important to us. What can we do for you today?
What else is on your mind?

dimensions.label: Responding to emotion (PEARLS)
dimensions.description: GREAT step R — respond to emotion using Partnership, Emotion, Apology/Acknowledgement, Respect, Legitimization, Support.

coaching.whyItMatters: Naming and responding to emotion (PEARLS) is the core of relationship-centered care.
examplePhrases: I can only imagine how hard this has been (Empathy).
We're going to face this together (Partnership).

dimensions.label: Assessment & plan (Treatment plan for the day)
dimensions.description: GREAT steps A and T — share the assessment and the day's plan in plain language.

coaching.whyItMatters: Sharing 'our plan today to address your concerns and ours' aligns the team and family on the same goals.
examplePhrases: Our plan today to address your concerns and ours is…
Let me explain that without the jargon.

dimensions.label: Closing
dimensions.description: Confirm understanding, summarize action items, and set expectations for return.

coaching.whyItMatters: Teach-back and clear action items prevent confusion after the team leaves the room.
examplePhrases: What questions do you have before we move on?
We'll be back this afternoon to check in.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician greet the patient (and family) and ask how they are feeling?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Warm greeting that opens the encounter.
exemplarQuotes: Good morning, how are you feeling today?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician introduce themselves/the team and their role as appropriate?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Identifies who they are on the team.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician ask what the patient/family's concerns are for the day?
exemplarQuotes: What can we do for you today?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician probe for additional concerns ('what else?')?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Asks again to surface further concerns.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician gather and include patient/family input? (N/A if no family present.)
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Invites the family into the conversation as appropriate.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician acknowledge and respond to emotion (a PEARLS statement)?
exemplarQuotes: I can see this is a shock.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician convey support, concern, and respect (verbally)?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Respectful, supportive tone toward patient and family.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician respond well to patient/family questions?
Did the clinician use straightforward language and avoid jargon?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Explains medical concepts in everyday terms.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician share the plan for the day, addressing the patient's concerns and the team's?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: States a clear plan for the day tied to the concerns raised.
exemplarQuotes: Our plan today to address your concerns and ours is…

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician include the patient/family in the discussion and decisions?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Invites input rather than dictating the plan.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician ensure patient/family understanding (teach-back or inviting questions)?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Checks understanding before leaving the room.
exemplarQuotes: What questions do you have before we move on?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician clarify or summarize the action items / what happens next?
Did the clinician set expectations for return or how to reach the team?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Tells the patient/family when the team will return or how to get help.
